bibliographic_data.title: Multimode Control Attacks on Elections
bibliographic_data.abstract: In 1992, Bartholdi, Tovey, and Trick opened the study of control attacks on elections—attempts to improve the election outcome by such actions as adding/deleting candidates or voters. That work has led to many results on how algorithms can be used to find attacks on elections and how complexity-theoretic hardness results can be used as shields against attacks. However, all the work in this line has assumed that the attacker employs just a single type of attack. In this paper, we model and study the case in which the attacker launches a multipronged (i.e., multimode) attack. We do so to more realistically capture the richness of real-life settings. For example, an attacker might simultaneously try to suppress some voters, attract new voters into the election, and introduce a spoiler candidate. Our model provides a unified framework for such varied attacks, and by constructing polynomial-time multiprong attack algorithms we prove that for various election systems even such concerted, flexible attacks can be perfectly planned in deterministic polynomial time.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper models and studies the case of multipronged (multimode) control attacks on elections, providing a unified framework for varied attacks that can be planned in polynomial time.
core_content.key_methodology: Constructing polynomial-time algorithms to find optimal multiprong attack strategies.
outcomes: Developed a unified framework for modeling and analyzing multipronged control attacks on elections.
Proved that even concerted, flexible attacks can be perfectly planned in deterministic polynomial time.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In 1992, Bartholdi, Tovey, and Trick initiated research aimed at protecting elections from various attacking actions intended to skew the election’s results. Their work focused on single-type attack scenarios but did not consider combined attacks. This paper addresses that gap by modeling multipronged control attacks, which involve using multiple standard attack types simultaneously. The authors provide a unified framework for analyzing such attacks and demonstrate that even complex, flexible attacks can be planned efficiently in polynomial time.
